seoTitle: How to Get an Incoming Webhook URL on Slack
metaDescription: Generate a Slack incoming webhook URL from the developer dashboard so external apps can post messages to a specific channel.
intro: Connecting external apps or automated scripts to Slack requires a secure webhook endpoint. This guide covers creating a new app, enabling Incoming Webhooks, and copying the finalized URL for your integration.

steps.heading: Create a new app
steps.body: Navigate to the Slack API dashboard and click 'Create New App' to begin.

steps.heading: Build from scratch
steps.body: Select 'From Scratch', name your app, and choose your target workspace.

steps.heading: Enable Incoming Webhooks
steps.body: Scroll to the Features section and click on 'Incoming Webhooks' to open its configuration ribbon.

steps.heading: Activate the feature and add a webhook
steps.body: Toggle the activation slider to On, then click 'Add New Webhook to Workspace' and choose a target channel.

steps.heading: Copy your webhook URL
steps.body: Click the Copy button next to your newly minted URL to save it to your clipboard for use in external scripts.

faq.question: How is this different from Slack's Workflow Builder automations?
faq.answer: Incoming Webhooks are developer-controlled endpoints for external systems, while Workflow Builder handles automations built entirely inside Slack.

faq.question: Can I test my webhook without writing code?
faq.answer: Yes, you can send a sample JSON payload from a terminal using curl to verify the message posts correctly.

faq.question: Is the webhook URL reusable across multiple channels?
faq.answer: No, each webhook URL is tied to a single specific channel you selected during setup.

faq.question: What should I do if my webhook URL is exposed?
faq.answer: Remove the compromised webhook from your app's Incoming Webhooks settings and create a new one immediately.
